document_metadata.title: Whiteland girls soccer hoping to keep magical season going
document_metadata.first_words: History doesn’t always play out in the most-familiar settings in front of the most-familiar faces. And so it was last Wednesday as Whiteland secured the program’s first outright Mid-State Conference championship with a 2-0 victory at Mooresville. Goals by forwards Rhianna Hardy and Kinley Waalkens were the difference as the Warriors punctuated a 6-0-1 league ledger – the lone hint of imperfection being a 2-2 tie at Greenwood in late-August. Whiteland’s previous peak was going 5-1 against Mid-State opposition in 2017 and tying for the title with Plainfield and Mooresville. Senior midfielder Madison Fleury, a four-year

chunks.content: History doesn’t always play out in the most-familiar settings in front of the most-familiar faces. And so it was last Wednesday as Whiteland secured the program’s first outright Mid-State Conference championship with a 2-0 victory at Mooresville. Goals by forwards Rhianna Hardy and Kinley Waalkens were the difference as the Warriors punctuated a 6-0-1 league ledger – the lone hint of imperfection being a 2-2 tie at Greenwood in late-August. Whiteland’s previous peak was going 5-1 against Mid-State opposition in 2017 and tying for the title with Plainfield and Mooresville. Senior midfielder Madison Fleury, a four-year starter whose 51 career goals places her in the top three in program history in that category behind Jordan Francis (90) and Emma Gill (54), wasn’t feeling the need to share. “We’ve been getting better every single year. I was not putting us in a position of sharing it. I wanted it all for ourselves,” said Fleury, who’ll help lead the Warriors (11-4-1) against Franklin (8-6-2) on Tuesday in the first round of the Class 3A sectional at Greenwood. “We work hard, and everything from the past years have helped us get to where we’re at.”
chunks.content_original: History doesn’t always play out in the most-familiar settings in front of the most-familiar faces. And so it was last Wednesday as Whiteland secured the program’s first outright Mid-State Conference championship with a 2-0 victory at Mooresville. Goals by forwards Rhianna Hardy and Kinley Waalkens were the difference as the Warriors punctuated a 6-0-1 league ledger – the lone hint of imperfection being a 2-2 tie at Greenwood in late-August. Whiteland’s previous peak was going 5-1 against Mid-State opposition in 2017 and tying for the title with Plainfield and Mooresville. Senior midfielder Madison Fleury, a four-year starter whose 51 career goals places her in the top three in program history in that category behind Jordan Francis (90) and Emma Gill (54), wasn’t feeling the need to share. “We’ve been getting better every single year. I was not putting us in a position of sharing it. I wanted it all for ourselves,” said Fleury, who’ll help lead the Warriors (11-4-1) against Franklin (8-6-2) on Tuesday in the first round of the Class 3A sectional at Greenwood. “We work hard, and everything from the past years have helped us get to where we’re at.”

chunks.content: It’s been a steady climb back for a program that sputtered to a 1-15-1 record in 2018. A season later, with then-first-year coach Nick Magdalinos at the helm, Whiteland posted a 1-15 mark before recording six victories the following fall and gradually progressing from there. Now they’re flying solo at the top. “This team is the closest in terms of chemistry that I think I’ve ever coached,” Magdalinos said. “They root for each other. “And they’re so unselfish. It doesn’t matter who scores the goals or who gets the assists. When someone has a bad game, someone else steps up.” Whiteland’s depth allows it to utilize the talents of between 16-18 players per match. The team’s starting midfielders are Fleury (22 goals), Bethany Floyd, Maddie McGuire, Ava Fagan and Kaelynn Crawford. Hardy (15 goals), a senior, and Waalkens (9 goals, 13 assists), a sophomore, are the forwards, with Alexa Lee, Ashley Cooprider and Hailey Hillhouse (5 goals) starting as defenders. In goal for Whiteland with a total of 77 saves this season is sophomore Keiana Bostock. Senior Elnora Stevenson (13 goals) and freshman Kiaya Barnard are the first players off the bench. Over the course of the past four seasons, Whiteland has very quietly pieced together an overall record of 42-20-3 (20-5-3 in the Mid-State). The program has yet to capture a sectional championship. However, the possibility of meeting perennial power and defending 3A runner-up Center Grove (15-2) in Saturday’s championship match still exists. “I wasn’t surprised to see this group having the success they’ve had because they’ve been building toward this,” Whiteland athletics director David Edens said. “Nick has built a culture, and this is such a great group of seniors.”

markdown_content:
History doesn’t always play out in the most-familiar settings in front of the most-familiar faces.

And so it was last Wednesday as Whiteland secured the program’s first outright Mid-State Conference championship with a 2-0 victory at Mooresville.

Goals by forwards Rhianna Hardy and Kinley Waalkens were the difference as the Warriors punctuated a 6-0-1 league ledger – the lone hint of imperfection being a 2-2 tie at Greenwood in late-August.

Whiteland’s previous peak was going 5-1 against Mid-State opposition in 2017 and tying for the title with Plainfield and Mooresville.

Senior midfielder Madison Fleury, a four-year starter whose 51 career goals places her in the top three in program history in that category behind Jordan Francis (90) and Emma Gill (54), wasn’t feeling the need to share.

“We’ve been getting better every single year. I was not putting us in a position of sharing it. I wanted it all for ourselves,” said Fleury, who’ll help lead the Warriors (11-4-1) against Franklin (8-6-2) on Tuesday in the first round of the Class 3A sectional at Greenwood.

“We work hard, and everything from the past years have helped us get to where we’re at.”

It’s been a steady climb back for a program that sputtered to a 1-15-1 record in 2018. A season later, with then-first-year coach Nick Magdalinos at the helm, Whiteland posted a 1-15 mark before recording six victories the following fall and gradually progressing from there.

Now they’re flying solo at the top.

“This team is the closest in terms of chemistry that I think I’ve ever coached,” Magdalinos said. “They root for each other.

“And they’re so unselfish. It doesn’t matter who scores the goals or who gets the assists. When someone has a bad game, someone else steps up.”

Whiteland’s depth allows it to utilize the talents of between 16-18 players per match.

The team’s starting midfielders are Fleury (22 goals), Bethany Floyd, Maddie McGuire, Ava Fagan and Kaelynn Crawford. Hardy (15 goals), a senior, and Waalkens (9 goals, 13 assists), a sophomore, are the forwards, with Alexa Lee, Ashley Cooprider and Hailey Hillhouse (5 goals) starting as defenders.

In goal for Whiteland with a total of 77 saves this season is sophomore Keiana Bostock. Senior Elnora Stevenson (13 goals) and freshman Kiaya Barnard are the first players off the bench.

Over the course of the past four seasons, Whiteland has very quietly pieced together an overall record of 42-20-3 (20-5-3 in the Mid-State).

The program has yet to capture a sectional championship. However, the possibility of meeting perennial power and defending 3A runner-up Center Grove (15-2) in Saturday’s championship match still exists.

“I wasn’t surprised to see this group having the success they’ve had because they’ve been building toward this,” Whiteland athletics director David Edens said.

“Nick has built a culture, and this is such a great group of seniors.”
